confidence.disclosure: Imam Muslim ibn al-Hajjaj al-Naysaburi is the compiler of the second of the two Sahih collections that ground Sunni hadith literature. The early sources (Hakim al-Naysaburi, Dhahabi) record his Nishapuri origins, his travels across the central Islamic lands, his close association with Bukhari, and his death in Nishapur. Specific years for his earliest travels are reconstructed.

sources: Jonathan Brown, The Canonization of al-Bukhari and Muslim (2007)

journey.event: Born in Nishapur in Khurasan in 206 AH into a family of the Arab tribe of Qushayr. His father al-Hajjaj is a respected man and gives him an early religious education.
Begins formal hadith studies in Nishapur with the local masters at the age of about twelve, an early start that he later attributes to his father's encouragement.
Performs the hajj as a young man and studies with the Meccan hadith masters. After the pilgrimage he begins his own systematic rihla in search of hadith.
Travels through the major centres of Iraq — Kufa, Basra, and Baghdad — collecting from the leading hadith scholars and verifying chains.
Continues his rihla through Syria, gathering material in Damascus, Hims, Caesarea, and the towns of the Syrian coast.
Travels to Egypt to collect hadith from the Egyptian masters, including the leading Maliki teachers who had studied with the pupils of Imam Malik in Medina.

Returns to Nishapur and meets Bukhari, who has begun lecturing in the city. Muslim attaches himself to Bukhari as a student and admirer, kissing the older master's forehead and saying: 'Let me kiss your feet, master of masters and physician of hadith.'

When the Nishapuri controversy erupts and al-Dhuhli denounces Bukhari, Muslim — almost alone — refuses to abandon Bukhari and returns Dhuhli's hadith books to him as a public break with his former teacher. The act ends his standing with the Nishapuri establishment but cements his loyalty to Bukhari.

Composes his own Al-Jami al-Sahih, drawing from his vast notes after years of travel. Unlike Bukhari, he organises the chapters strictly by topic and gives all the variant chains for each hadith — features that later commentators will praise.

Dies in Nishapur in Rajab 261 AH (May 875) and is buried in the Nasrabad quarter of the city. The Sahih Muslim becomes, alongside Sahih al-Bukhari, the most authoritative hadith collection in Sunni Islam, and the two together are known as the Sahihayn.
